title: The drawer of saved letters
summary: Most of us keep a few old letters for decades but delete thousands of emails without thinking. The essay asks what we actually buy when we save everything.
body:
Open the drawer in your parents' house — the one with the keys, the small scissors, the rubber bands — and somewhere near the back you will probably find a few letters. Not many. Five, eight, sometimes a small bundle tied with string. They are not the most important letters of their lives. Often nobody remembers exactly why these particular ones were kept and others were thrown away. But there they are, twenty or forty years later.

Now think about your email. You have, on average, around fifteen thousand emails in your account. When you change provider, you migrate them. When the inbox gets too full, you delete by date — 'older than three years, all gone'. You feel nothing.

What is the difference?

A letter takes work. Someone sat down, chose paper, found a pen, wrote with care, walked it to a post box. The handwriting itself shows the day they wrote it — the careful version, the rushed version, the night they were happy, the morning they were tired. None of this survives in an email, which looks the same whether it took six seconds or six hours to write.

A letter is also unique. There is one copy. If you throw it away, it is gone. An email exists on a hundred servers; deleting your copy doesn't delete it. So a letter quietly asks: do you want to be the one who kept this, or the one who didn't?

There is something else, too. A letter has weight. You can hold a stack of them. You can flick through them in a single afternoon. You can read three sentences and stop, and the rest waits politely on the kitchen table. Fifteen thousand emails do not wait. They scroll past. You cannot 'put them down'.

This is not an argument that paper is better than pixels. Many of the kindest, most honest words written in the last twenty years have been emails. But we should at least notice the trade. We saved every word, and in saving every word, we made every word weigh less.

If you are over a certain age, you may still have a letter from someone who has died. If you are under a certain age, you may not. That gap is small but real. Perhaps the answer is not to start writing letters again — most of us won't — but to print one email a year. The one we wouldn't want to lose. Put it in a drawer, near the keys.

annotations.meaningVi: bó nhỏ, gói nhỏ (đồ buộc lại)
di chuyển (dữ liệu) sang nơi mới
chữ viết tay
chồng (đồ vật xếp lên nhau)
sự đánh đổi (ở đây không phải là buôn bán)
vứt đi, bỏ đi (phrasal verb: bỏ bỏ thứ gì không cần nữa)
tính trung bình (discourse marker dùng khi nêu con số ước tính)
hòm thư (nơi bỏ thư để gửi đi)
độc nhất vô nhị, không có bản sao nào khác
lật nhanh qua (đọc lướt, xem lướt tài liệu)
cuộn qua (trên màn hình), trượt qua mà không dừng lại
đặt xuống, ngừng đọc (dùng cho sách/thư; ngụ ý chủ động dừng lại)
điểm ảnh; ở đây dùng hoán dụ để chỉ nội dung kỹ thuật số/màn hình
có trọng lượng ít hơn; ở đây nghĩa bóng: ít giá trị/ý nghĩa hơn
một độ tuổi nhất định (cách diễn đạt mơ hồ có chủ ý, tránh nêu con số cụ thể)
ít nhất là, tối thiểu (discourse marker nhượng bộ, nhấn mạnh điều tối thiểu cần làm)
nhà cung cấp (dịch vụ), ví dụ: nhà cung cấp dịch vụ email
dây thun / dây chun (dùng để buộc đồ)

annotations.phrase: waits politely on the kitchen table
annotations.meaningVi: kiên nhẫn nằm chờ trên bàn bếp — nhân cách hoá thư từ, tương phản với email cuộn qua màn hình
cũ hơn ba năm — cụm so sánh thường dùng khi nói về thời hạn lưu trữ dữ liệu

grammarNotes.title: Defining a person with 'the one who…'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Cấu trúc 'the one who...' (người mà...) dùng để xác định/định nghĩa một người dựa trên hành động họ đã làm. Trong bài: 'do you want to be the one who kept this, or the one who didn't?' — bạn muốn là người đã giữ lá thư, hay người đã không giữ?
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use 'the one who…' (or 'someone who…') to define a person by what they did. From the essay: 'do you want to be the one who kept this, or the one who didn't?' The clause after 'who' identifies which person you mean. Form: the one + who + verb.

cloze.template:
Open the {{b1}} in your parents' house — the one with the keys, the small scissors, the rubber bands — and somewhere near the back you will probably find a few letters. Not many. Five, eight, sometimes a small {{b2}} tied with string. They are not the most important letters of their lives. Often nobody remembers exactly why these particular ones were kept and others were {{b3}} away. But there they are, twenty or forty years later.

Now think about your email. You have, on average, around fifteen thousand emails in your account. When you change provider, you {{b4}} them. When the inbox gets too full, you delete by date — 'older than three years, all gone'. You feel nothing.

What is the difference?

A letter takes work. Someone sat down, chose paper, found a pen, wrote with care, walked it to a post box. The {{b5}} itself shows the day they wrote it — the careful version, the rushed version, the night they were happy, the morning they were tired. None of this survives in an email, which looks the same whether it took six seconds or six hours to write.

A letter is also {{b6}}. There is one copy. If you throw it away, it is gone. An email exists on a hundred servers; deleting your copy doesn't delete it. So a letter quietly asks: do you want to be the one who {{b7}} this, or the one who didn't?

There is something else, too. A letter has {{b8}}. You can hold a {{b9}} of them. You can flick through them in a single afternoon. You can read three sentences and stop, and the rest waits politely on the kitchen table. Fifteen thousand emails do not wait. They {{b10}} past. You cannot 'put them down'.

This is not an argument that paper is better than pixels. Many of the kindest, most honest words written in the last twenty years have been emails. But we should at least notice the {{b11}}. We saved every word, and in saving every word, we made every word {{b12}} less.

If you are over a certain age, you may still have a letter from someone who has died. If you are under a certain age, you may not. That gap is small but real. Perhaps the answer is not to start writing letters again — most of us won't — but to {{b13}} one email a year. The one we wouldn't want to lose. Put it in a drawer, near the keys.

blanks.explanation: The opening image is a drawer — described as 'the one with the keys, the small scissors, the rubber bands'.
'A small bundle tied with string' — a bundle is letters held together.
Some letters were kept and others were 'thrown away'.
When you change provider you 'migrate' your emails — move them to the new place.
The handwriting itself reveals the mood and time of writing — not the paper or stamp.
'There is one copy' — that is what 'unique' means here.
'Do you want to be the one who kept this, or the one who didn't?'
'A letter has weight' — you can hold and feel it.
A stack of letters — many letters piled together.
Emails 'scroll past' on a screen — you cannot put them down.
Notice the trade — what we lost in exchange for being able to save everything.
We made every word 'weigh less' — the writer continues the 'letter has weight' image.
Print one email a year — turn one important email back into something physical.

translationVi:
Mở ngăn kéo trong nhà bố mẹ bạn — cái ngăn có chùm chìa khóa, cái kéo nhỏ, mấy sợi dây thun — và đâu đó ở phía trong, bạn sẽ tìm thấy vài lá thư. Không nhiều. Năm, tám lá, có khi một bó nhỏ buộc bằng dây. Đó không phải là những lá thư quan trọng nhất trong đời họ. Thường thì chẳng ai nhớ chính xác vì sao những lá thư này được giữ lại còn các lá khác bị vứt đi. Nhưng chúng vẫn ở đó, hai mươi hay bốn mươi năm sau.

Bây giờ hãy nghĩ đến email của bạn. Trung bình, bạn có khoảng mười lăm nghìn email trong tài khoản. Khi đổi nhà cung cấp, bạn chuyển hết chúng sang. Khi hộp thư đầy, bạn xóa theo ngày — 'cũ hơn ba năm, xóa hết'. Bạn không cảm thấy gì.

Khác biệt nằm ở đâu?

Một lá thư đòi hỏi công sức. Có người ngồi xuống, chọn giấy, tìm bút, viết cẩn thận, đi bộ ra hòm thư. Chính chữ viết tay cũng cho thấy ngày họ viết — phiên bản cẩn thận, phiên bản vội vàng, buổi tối họ vui, buổi sáng họ mệt. Không gì trong số đó còn lại trong một email, vốn trông y hệt nhau dù viết trong sáu giây hay sáu giờ.

Một lá thư cũng là độc bản. Chỉ có một bản. Nếu vứt đi, là mất hẳn. Một email tồn tại trên hàng trăm máy chủ; xóa bản của bạn không xóa được nó. Vì vậy lá thư lặng lẽ hỏi: bạn muốn là người đã giữ nó lại, hay là người đã không giữ?

Còn một điều nữa. Lá thư có trọng lượng. Bạn có thể cầm cả một chồng. Bạn có thể lật xem trong một buổi chiều. Bạn có thể đọc ba câu rồi dừng, và phần còn lại kiên nhẫn nằm trên bàn bếp. Mười lăm nghìn email thì không kiên nhẫn. Chúng trượt qua. Bạn không thể 'đặt chúng xuống'.

Đây không phải là lập luận rằng giấy hơn pixel. Nhiều câu chữ tử tế và chân thật nhất hai mươi năm qua đều là email. Nhưng ít nhất chúng ta nên nhận ra cái đánh đổi. Chúng ta đã lưu mọi lời, và bằng việc lưu mọi lời, chúng ta đã khiến mỗi lời nhẹ đi.

Nếu bạn đã đến một độ tuổi nào đó, có thể bạn vẫn còn giữ một lá thư từ một người đã khuất. Nếu bạn trẻ hơn, có thể không. Khoảng cách ấy nhỏ, nhưng có thật. Có lẽ câu trả lời không phải là quay lại viết thư tay — hầu hết chúng ta sẽ không — mà là mỗi năm in ra một email. Cái mà chúng ta không muốn mất. Đặt nó vào ngăn kéo, gần chùm chìa khóa.

questions.prompt: What does the writer say you can usually find at the back of an old drawer at your parents' house?
questions.explanation: 'You will probably find a few letters. Not many. Five, eight, sometimes a small bundle tied with string.'
questions.hint: Look at the very first paragraph.

questions.prompt: How does the writer describe our behaviour with old emails?
options: We carefully read every one before deleting
We delete thousands by date without feeling anything
We keep them forever and never delete
questions.explanation: 'You delete by date — "older than three years, all gone". You feel nothing.'

questions.prompt: According to the writer, what does handwriting show that an email does not?
options: The colour of the writer's clothes
The mood and conditions of the day the letter was written
questions.explanation: 'The careful version, the rushed version, the night they were happy, the morning they were tired.'

questions.prompt: Why does the writer call a letter 'unique'?
options: There is only one copy of it
questions.explanation: 'There is one copy. If you throw it away, it is gone.'

questions.prompt: Why doesn't deleting an email truly delete it?
options: The post office still has it
It exists on a hundred servers; only your copy is gone
Emails cannot be deleted by law
questions.explanation: 'An email exists on a hundred servers; deleting your copy doesn't delete it.'

questions.prompt: What does the writer mean by 'a letter has weight'?
options: You can physically hold and pause over them; emails 'scroll past' instead
Letters take up too much room
questions.explanation: 'You can read three sentences and stop, and the rest waits politely on the kitchen table.'
questions.hint: Look at paragraph 6 — what can you do with a stack that you can't do with emails?

questions.prompt: What 'trade' does the writer want us to notice?
options: We bought email accounts that cost us money
We saved every word, and in doing so, made every word weigh less
questions.explanation: 'We saved every word, and in saving every word, we made every word weigh less.'
questions.hint: Look at paragraph 7 for the word 'trade'.

questions.prompt: What 'small but real' gap does the writer point out between older and younger readers?
options: Older readers may still have a letter from someone who has died; younger ones may not
questions.explanation: 'If you are over a certain age, you may still have a letter from someone who has died. If you are under a certain age, you may not.'

questions.prompt: What concrete small action does the writer suggest?
options: Print one email a year — the one you wouldn't want to lose — and keep it in a drawer
Send a letter to your parents
questions.explanation: 'To print one email a year. The one we wouldn't want to lose. Put it in a drawer, near the keys.'
questions.hint: Look at the closing two sentences.

questions.prompt: Which sentence best captures the writer's main idea?
options: Emails are always worse than letters.
We should notice that being able to save everything has made each word weigh less.
Everyone should write a letter today.
questions.explanation: The writer explicitly says it is 'not an argument that paper is better than pixels' but a call to notice the trade.
questions.hint: Look at the start of paragraph 7.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The writer suggests that 'saving every word made every word weigh less'. Think of something else in your own life that you have been able to save or store unlimited copies of — photos, songs, messages. Has having more of it made each one feel more important, or less?
